title: Backend stack: Java/Spring Boot (not Supabase-direct)
description: DECISION (leaning, finalize at Phase 2). Backend = Java/Spring Boot instead of the handoff's Supabase-direct default. Rationale: solo dev is a Spring Boot expert; the domain is heavy (mesocycle volume calc w/ provenance, pattern engine, cross-load, ~69 entities, scheduled jobs) and fits Spring's strengths (JPA/Hibernate, Liquibase, Spring Security JWT, @Scheduled/Quartz, transactions). Phase 2: frontend mock-hooks swap to Spring Boot REST API -> Postgres (same hook interface; frontend unchanged). Phase 3 AI: Spring AI (multi-provider LLM, pgvector vector store, RAG, function calling, chat memory); optional Python sidecar only if multi-agent graph orchestration gets complex. Deploy: Vercel (frontend) + Spring Boot container on Cloud Run (scale-to-zero) + managed Postgres (Neon/Supabase-DB). Trade-off accepted: lose all-serverless-free simplicity; re-derive schema in Liquibase/JPA from 04-data-model + Phase-3 arch doc instead of using handoff Supabase DDL/RLS/Edge blueprints. Does NOT affect Phase 1 (mock). Repo structure (root frontend vs frontend/+backend/ split) to be decided at Phase 2.

title: Parity decision: NOVA palette — NovaPanel (MealScore) uses unified NovaDot hues, not prototype meal-score.jsx palette
description: The prototype is internally inconsistent: fuel-recipes.jsx NovaDot and meal-score.jsx NovaPanel use DIFFERENT NOVA-group color palettes. We unified to ONE palette (NovaDot hues) in src/data/nova.ts NOVA_META as a productionization consolidation. Consequence: the MealScoreSheet NovaPanel colors differ from the prototype's NovaPanel. Also NOVA_META[1] is a hardcoded hex (#34D399) that does not theme-switch in light mode (var(--cat-response) would). Revisit at the parity checkpoint: either accept the consolidation (recommended) or split into two palettes for exact per-screen parity.

value: macOS git-hook gotcha: GNU 'timeout' is NOT installed on this machine, so the beads pre-commit/pre-push hooks run 'bd hooks run' WITHOUT a timeout guard. If multiple bd processes contend for the Dolt lock (e.g. bd init's own commit colliding with manual git commits), the hook hangs INDEFINITELY and git commit/push silently stalls. Fix when it happens: kill the stuck 'bd hooks run' + 'bd export' processes (ps aux | grep bd) to clear the lock, then retry — a single serialized commit works fine. Permanent fix: brew install coreutils and put its gnubin on PATH so 'timeout' exists.

Run & visually verify Mezo locally: 'pnpm dev' -> http://localhost:5173 (Vite, no custom port). It is a mobile PWA designed at 440x956 (iPhone 17 Pro Max) — preview/screenshot at that viewport for realism; widen the browser to see the desktop iPhone-mockup frame. Playwright screenshot scripts: import { chromium } from '@playwright/test' (NOT 'playwright' — that package is not installed) and run the .mjs FROM the project dir so node resolves node_modules. The dev server registers NO service worker, so if the browser shows stale behavior it is a stale tab or an OLD build's SW (dist/sw.js) — hard-reload or test in incognito. To verify CSS transitions/animations, sample getComputedStyle(el).transform at MULTIPLE timestamps (~every 30ms) — a single mid-sample at the full value masks an instant jump.

People data: relationPatterns[].involves uses person IDs (pp-adam, pp-reka, pp-mark) but peopleSummary.attention[].person and ritualUpcoming.attendees use display NAMES (Réka, Ádám). PeopleView (Task 10) must resolve involves via people.find(p=>p.id===...) while rendering attention/attendees names directly. Matches data.js faithfully.

Sheet (src/components/ui/Sheet.tsx) architecture & behavior: renders via createPortal into '.phone-screen' (fallback document.body) so position:absolute;bottom:0 anchors to the DEVICE viewport and the backdrop covers the tab bar — NOT the scrolling .screen-content it is mounted inside (original bug: CheckInSheet rendered inside scroll content, so the sheet sat mid-page). Entrance = pure CSS @keyframes sheet-rise (slide up) — reliable. Drag-to-dismiss on .sheet-handle-zone (close if dy>min(120px,28% height) OR velocity>0.5px/ms; else snap back). CLOSE is animated on EVERY path (backdrop, Escape, drag, in-sheet X/Save): requestClose() slides down + fades backdrop ~300ms then calls onClose. The animated close reaches children via RENDER-PROP: children may be (close)=>ReactNode; CheckInSheet & QuickInputSheet use {(close)=>(...)} and call close() from X/Save/commit. CONSEQUENCE: onClose is ASYNC now — tests asserting closure must use waitFor (Sheet.test, QuickInputSheet.test, navigation.test). The close depends on the reflow/rAF trick in memory key sheet-close-animation-gotcha-setting-el-style-animation.

PhoneFrame (.phone in prototype.css) is a DESKTOP-ONLY preview mockup sized to iPhone 17 Pro Max (440x956, island 124x37, home bar 134x5). A media query (max-width:519px OR display-mode:standalone/fullscreen/minimal-ui) strips the bezel/island/fake-statusbar/home-indicator and goes full-bleed with env(safe-area-inset-*). index.html viewport uses viewport-fit=cover. So on real phones the OS chrome is used, not the mockup.

Sheet CLOSE animation gotcha: setting el.style.animation='none' + transition + transform in ONE sync tick makes the browser coalesce the writes and JUMP to the end state (instant disappear) instead of animating — Chromium-headless tolerated it but real browsers (e.g. Safari) did not. FIX in requestClose(): animation='none'; void el.offsetHeight (force reflow to commit current pos as start frame); set transition; then set transform:translateY(100%) inside requestAnimationFrame (next frame). Verified gradual ease-out curve 0->460px over ~300ms. Entrance slide-up is a pure CSS @keyframes (sheet-rise) so it never had this problem. LESSON: when verifying animations in Playwright, sample the transform at MULTIPLE timestamps to confirm a gradual curve — a single mid-sample at full height can mask an instant jump (it fooled me once).
